kids.question: Who did John write this letter to?
kids.explanation: 3 John 1:1 — "The elder unto the wellbeloved Gaius, whom I love in the truth."

kids.question: What did John say he had no greater joy than?
kids.option_a: Hearing that his children walk in truth
kids.explanation: 3 John 1:4 — "I have no greater joy than to hear that my children walk in truth."

kids.question: Who was a bad example in this letter because he loved to be first?
kids.explanation: 3 John 1:9 — "Diotrephes, who loveth to have the preeminence among them."

teens.question: What good thing did John praise Gaius for?
teens.option_a: Showing hospitality to travelling brethren and strangers
teens.explanation: 3 John 1:5 — "thou doest faithfully whatsoever thou doest to the brethren, and to strangers."

teens.question: What did Diotrephes do to those who wanted to welcome the brethren?
teens.option_a: He cast them out of the church
teens.explanation: 3 John 1:10 — "neither doth he himself receive the brethren, and forbiddeth them that would, and casteth them out of the church."

teens.question: What did John say about Demetrius?
teens.option_a: He had a good report from all men and from the truth itself
teens.explanation: 3 John 1:12 — "Demetrius hath good report of all men, and of the truth itself."

teens.question: What does John say about imitating?
teens.option_a: Follow not that which is evil, but that which is good
teens.option_b: Imitate the leaders of the synagogue
teens.option_c: Follow the customs of the Romans
teens.explanation: 3 John 1:11 — "Beloved, follow not that which is evil, but that which is good."

teens.question: Why did the travelling brethren go forth?
teens.option_a: For the sake of the Name (of Jesus), taking nothing of the Gentiles
teens.explanation: 3 John 1:7 — "Because that for his name's sake they went forth, taking nothing of the Gentiles."

adults.question: What does John's opening wish for Gaius reveal about his pastoral concern?
adults.option_a: He wishes that Gaius may prosper and be in health, even as his soul prospereth — showing concern for whole-person wellbeing
adults.option_c: He commands Gaius to fast and pray
adults.option_d: He asks Gaius to send money
adults.explanation: 3 John 1:2 — "I wish above all things that thou mayest prosper and be in health, even as thy soul prospereth."

adults.question: What does John say is the duty of believers toward travelling missionaries?
adults.option_a: We ought to receive and support such, that we might be fellowhelpers to the truth
adults.option_b: We should test them for a year before helping
adults.option_c: We should send them to the synagogue for support
adults.option_d: We should only support those from our own church
adults.explanation: 3 John 1:8 — "We therefore ought to receive such, that we might be fellowhelpers to the truth."

adults.question: What three specific charges does John level against Diotrephes?
adults.option_a: He loves preeminence, speaks malicious words against the apostles, and refuses to receive the brethren while casting out those who do
adults.option_b: He steals money, lies about his credentials, and worships idols
adults.option_c: He denies the resurrection, rejects baptism, and forsakes the assembly
adults.option_d: He teaches false doctrine, forbids prayer, and burns Scripture
adults.explanation: 3 John 1:9-10 — "Diotrephes, who loveth to have the preeminence… prating against us with malicious words: and not content therewith, neither doth he himself receive the brethren, and forbiddeth them that would, and casteth them out."

adults.question: What theological principle does John state about doing good and doing evil?
adults.option_a: "He that doeth good is of God: but he that doeth evil hath not seen God"
adults.option_b: "Good and evil are equal paths to knowledge"
adults.option_c: "Evil is merely the absence of good"
adults.option_d: "Only God can truly distinguish good from evil"
adults.explanation: 3 John 1:11 — "He that doeth good is of God: but he that doeth evil hath not seen God."

adults.question: What is significant about Demetrius having testimony from "the truth itself"?
adults.option_a: It means his life and doctrine are validated not only by human witnesses but by the objective standard of divine truth
adults.option_b: The truth is a person who wrote a letter about him
adults.option_c: He passed a written examination on Scripture
adults.option_d: He was recognized by the Roman senate
adults.explanation: 3 John 1:12 — "Demetrius hath good report of all men, and of the truth itself: yea, and we also bear record."

adults.question: What does John say he wrote to the church, and what happened?
adults.option_a: He wrote to the church, but Diotrephes would not receive them (the letter/messengers)
adults.option_b: He wrote asking for money but was refused
adults.option_c: He wrote to schedule a visit but weather delayed him
adults.option_d: He wrote a doctrinal treatise that was lost
adults.explanation: 3 John 1:9 — "I wrote unto the church: but Diotrephes, who loveth to have the preeminence among them, receiveth us not."

adults.question: How does John plan to address the situation with Diotrephes?
adults.option_a: "If I come, I will remember his deeds which he doeth" — indicating personal apostolic confrontation
adults.option_b: He will write a stronger letter
adults.option_c: He will send Peter to deal with it
adults.option_d: He will excommunicate him from afar
adults.explanation: 3 John 1:10 — "if I come, I will remember his deeds which he doeth, prating against us with malicious words."

adults.question: How does John close this letter, and what does it reveal about early church communication?
adults.option_a: He prefers face-to-face conversation over writing, showing the importance of personal presence in apostolic ministry
adults.option_b: He asks Gaius to burn the letter for safety
adults.option_c: He sends a formal blessing through the bishop
adults.option_d: He attaches a creedal statement for the church to sign
adults.explanation: 3 John 1:13-14 — "I had many things to write, but I will not with ink and pen write unto thee: But I trust I shall shortly see thee, and we shall speak face to face."
